**The Moon's Debt**

Long ago, the Moon was a wandering spirit who loved to dance across the dark sky. She twirled freely, caring for nothing but her own joy. The Ocean below watched her every night, mesmerized by her silver glow.

One evening, the Moon danced too close to Earth and stumbled, falling toward the ground. The Ocean, seeing his beloved about to shatter on the rocks, surged upward with all his might, catching her in his waves. The effort exhausted him, and he nearly emptied himself saving her.

The Moon, grateful but embarrassed, tried to flee back to the sky. But the Ocean called out, "Wait! If you leave, I will forget this night, and my sacrifice will mean nothing."

Moved by his words, the Moon made a promise: "I cannot stay, for the sky is my home. But I will never forget you. Twice each day, I will pull you toward me, lifting you high so you may touch the heavens where I dance."

And so it remains. The tides rise and fall as the Moon keeps her ancient promise, forever trying to reunite with the Ocean who saved her life.

**The Weaver of Stars**

Long ago, when the sky was empty and black, there lived a lonely weaver named Celeste who lived atop the highest mountain. Each night, she would sit at her loom, creating beautiful tapestries that no one would ever see, for she lived alone above the clouds.

One evening, a young traveler became lost in a terrible storm. He climbed the mountain seeking shelter and found Celeste's home. She welcomed him warmly and showed him her tapestries—each one more magnificent than the last, woven with threads that seemed to glow with inner light.

"Why do you hide such beauty here?" the traveler asked.

Celeste wept. "I create because I must, but what purpose does beauty serve if no one can enjoy it?"

The traveler had an idea. Together, they carried her tapestries outside and threw them into the sky. The glowing threads scattered across the darkness, each one becoming a star. The patterns she had woven became constellations, telling stories for all to see.

From that night forward, Celeste continued weaving, and whenever she completed a tapestry, she would cast it into the heavens. This is why new stars are born, and why, if you look carefully, you can see stories written in the sky.

Myth: **The Potter's Song**

In the beginning, there was only silence and clay. A potter named Aurora lived in the valley between sleeping mountains, shaping vessels of extraordinary beauty. Each pot, bowl, and vase was perfect, yet she felt something was missing.

One dawn, a songbird fell from the sky, its wing broken. Aurora took the bird inside and cared for it. As it healed, the bird sang melodies Aurora had never imagined—songs of joy, sorrow, triumph, and loss.

"Your vessels are beautiful," the bird said, "but they are silent. Beauty without voice is like a story never told."

Aurora understood. She asked the bird to sing into each vessel as she shaped it, filling the clay with music. When she fired these special pots in her kiln, something magical happened—the heat transformed the songs into colors. Blues held lullabies, reds carried battle hymns, greens whispered forest songs.

People came from distant lands to carry Aurora's singing pots home. When they poured water or held them close, the vessels would hum softly, releasing their trapped melodies into the world.

This is why pottery has such varied, mysterious colors, and why certain pieces seem to sing to our souls—they remember Aurora's gift, when music and clay became one.
